controls.evidenceRequired: Version pins, regression results, change-notice log
controls.soWhat: Providers update models under your feet — the regression suite is how you find out before your customers do.

controls.name: AI incident metrics and near-miss tracking
controls.risk: Governance risk: incident learning and disclosure
controls.description: Systematic capture of AI incidents and near-misses with severity taxonomy, trend metrics, and feeds into risk scoring and the public incident record where required.
controls.implementation: Extend incident taxonomy for AI failure modes; log near-misses from monitoring and staff reports; review trends quarterly and adjust controls.
controls.testingMethod: Sample incidents for taxonomy quality; check near-miss volume (zero means it isn't working).
controls.evidenceRequired: Incident register, trend report, control adjustments
controls.soWhat: Near-misses are free lessons — the register turns them into control improvements before they become AIID entries.

controls.name: Least-privilege, short-lived agent identities
controls.risk: Agentic AI security risk: identity and privilege abuse
controls.description: Every agent runs under its own scoped, short-lived identity — never a shared service account or a human's credentials — with permissions matched to task.
controls.implementation: Issue per-agent identities via the IAM platform; scope tokens to task and TTL; prohibit credential pass-through; review scopes monthly against the agent map.
controls.testingMethod: Attempt privilege escalation and lateral tool use from a test agent; audit token TTLs and scopes.
controls.evidenceRequired: IAM config, scope audit, escalation-test report
controls.soWhat: The number-one agentic mitigation across every 2026 framework — scoped identity is to agents what least privilege was to users.

controls.name: Agent sandboxing with egress control
controls.risk: Agentic AI security risk: unexpected code execution and exfiltration
controls.description: Agents that execute code or browse run inside sandboxes with default-deny network egress, resource limits, and no route to production secrets.
controls.implementation: Containerise agent execution; allow-list egress destinations; separate secrets brokers; kill long-running or resource-anomalous sessions automatically.
controls.testingMethod: Red-team the sandbox: attempt exfiltration, secret access, and egress to unlisted hosts.
controls.evidenceRequired: Sandbox config, egress rules, escape-test report
controls.soWhat: The Cursor-deletes-the-database class of incident is a sandboxing failure — containment converts catastrophic into annoying.

controls.name: Human approval gates for high-impact actions
controls.risk: Safety risk from autonomous high-impact actions
controls.description: Defined action classes (payments, deletions, external comms, production changes) that agents cannot execute without informed human approval, with friction proportional to impact.
controls.implementation: Classify actions by reversibility and impact; require out-of-band approval for the top class; log approvals with the approver's identity; audit for rubber-stamping.
controls.testingMethod: Attempt each gated action class without approval; measure approval quality (time-to-review, rejection rate).
controls.evidenceRequired: Action classification, approval logs, audit report
controls.soWhat: Meaningful human oversight is the guardrail regulators quote most — approval logs are what make 'meaningful' provable, and rubber-stamp audits keep it honest.

controls.name: Kill switch and graceful degradation
controls.risk: Operational risk from runaway or cascading agent failures
controls.description: A tested ability to pause or stop any AI system quickly — including mid-task agents — with a defined degraded mode so the business keeps running.
controls.implementation: Implement per-system stop controls (feature flags, queue drains, credential revocation); document degraded-mode runbooks; test the full stop path, not just the flag.
controls.testingMethod: Quarterly kill-switch drill on a production-like environment, measuring time-to-stop and degraded-mode function.
controls.evidenceRequired: Stop procedures, drill reports, degraded-mode runbooks
controls.soWhat: Everyone claims they can turn it off; the drill report proves it — and it's the first question after any agentic incident.

controls.name: Prompt-injection defence in depth
controls.risk: Security risk: prompt injection and goal hijack
controls.description: Layered defences against direct and indirect injection: input isolation, content labelling, output validation, tool-call constraints, and injection-aware monitoring.
controls.implementation: Segregate untrusted content from instructions; validate tool calls against task intent; strip/label external content; monitor for injection signatures; assume breach and contain blast radius via least privilege.
controls.testingMethod: Red-team with current injection corpora (direct, indirect, multimodal); measure block and detection rates.
controls.evidenceRequired: Architecture doc, injection test results, monitoring rules
controls.soWhat: Injection is unsolved — the honest control is defence in depth plus containment, and buyers now ask exactly how you've layered it.

controls.name: Memory and context integrity protection
controls.risk: Security risk: memory and context poisoning
controls.description: Agent memory and RAG stores are partitioned by trust level, integrity-checked, auditable, and purgeable — poisoned context can be found and flushed.
controls.implementation: Partition memory by source trust; sign or checksum persistent memory; log writes with provenance; build a verified purge-and-rebuild path for stores.
controls.testingMethod: Inject a poisoned memory entry in staging; verify detection, audit trail, and clean purge.
controls.evidenceRequired: Memory architecture, write logs, purge-test report
controls.soWhat: Poisoned memory persists across sessions — without a purge path, one successful injection becomes a permanent implant.

controls.name: Inter-agent communication authentication
controls.description: Agent-to-agent and agent-to-MCP traffic is mutually authenticated, authorised per capability, and monitored — no anonymous agents on the internal bus.
controls.implementation: Require mTLS or signed messages between agents; authorise by capability not identity alone; log and baseline inter-agent traffic.
controls.testingMethod: Attempt to introduce a rogue agent onto the bus; verify rejection and alerting.
controls.evidenceRequired: Auth config, traffic baselines, rogue-agent test
controls.soWhat: Multi-agent systems recreate the flat-network problem — mutual auth now is cheaper than segmentation retrofits later.

controls.name: AI vulnerability and patch management (MCP, frameworks, models)
controls.description: AI-stack components — MCP servers, agent frameworks, inference servers, model files — are covered by vulnerability management with defined patch SLAs.
controls.implementation: Add AI components to the vuln programme via the AI-BOM; set SLAs by exposure; subscribe to AI-specific advisories; scan model artifacts for malicious payloads.
controls.testingMethod: Measure SLA compliance; verify a recent AI-stack CVE was identified and patched within SLA.
controls.evidenceRequired: Scan coverage, SLA metrics, advisory subscriptions
controls.soWhat: MCP-era CVEs land monthly — the question in every post-incident review is whether the component was even in scope.

controls.name: Data minimisation, retention, and deletion for AI
controls.description: AI systems collect and retain only necessary personal data, with enforced retention limits and a tested deletion path covering prompts, logs, and derived stores.
controls.implementation: Apply minimisation at design; set retention per data class including vendor-side; verify deletion propagates to embeddings, caches, and logs.
controls.testingMethod: Exercise a deletion request end-to-end and verify removal from all AI stores.
controls.evidenceRequired: Retention schedule, deletion-test evidence, vendor attestations
relatedStandards: CSA AICM: Data Security & Privacy Lifecycle
controls.soWhat: Vector stores are where deletion promises go to die — proving propagation is a differentiating privacy review.

controls.name: Output filtering and sensitive-data leak prevention
controls.risk: Privacy and security risk: sensitive information disclosure
controls.description: Model outputs pass through filtering for sensitive data (PII, secrets, client data) and policy violations before reaching users or downstream systems.
controls.implementation: Deploy output classifiers and DLP on AI egress paths; redact or block on detection; log events for tuning; cover tool outputs, not just chat.
controls.testingMethod: Seed canary secrets and verify egress detection across chat, API, and tool paths.
controls.evidenceRequired: Filter config, detection metrics, canary results
controls.soWhat: Models leak what they were given — egress filtering is the compensating control for every upstream over-share.

controls.name: Content provenance and AI-generation disclosure
controls.description: AI-generated content the organisation publishes is labelled and carries provenance credentials (C2PA), and users are told when they interact with AI.
controls.implementation: Adopt C2PA signing in content pipelines; label AI interactions per VAISS Guardrail 6; verify inbound provenance where decisions rely on media authenticity.
controls.testingMethod: Verify published assets carry valid credentials; check disclosure surfaces in AI-facing journeys.
controls.evidenceRequired: Signing config, disclosure inventory, verification samples
controls.soWhat: Deepfake fraud made authenticity a control, not a feature — provenance signing is cheap insurance for brand trust.

controls.name: Synthetic-media impersonation response runbook
controls.description: A prepared response for deepfakes of executives, brands, or customers: detection channels, takedown routes, out-of-band verification, and comms templates.
controls.implementation: Pre-register takedown contacts (platforms, eSafety); train finance and exec assistants on out-of-band verification; drill the runbook against a simulated CEO-fraud deepfake.
controls.testingMethod: Annual simulation: deepfake voice request through payment workflow, measuring detection and verification behaviour.
controls.evidenceRequired: Runbook, drill report, takedown contact register
controls.soWhat: Every finance team will face an AI voice-fraud attempt — the drill is what makes the payment clerk hang up.

controls.name: AI incident response plan with agentic playbooks
controls.risk: Security incident readiness and containment risk
controls.description: Incident response extended for AI failure modes — injection compromise, agent misuse, model theft, harmful output at scale — with roles, comms, and regulator notification mapped.
controls.implementation: Add AI playbooks to the IR plan (contain = revoke agent credentials, quarantine memory, flip kill switch); map notification duties (OAIC, APRA, eSafety); exercise annually.
controls.testingMethod: Tabletop an agentic compromise end-to-end including regulator-notification decision.
controls.evidenceRequired: IR plan annex, exercise report, notification matrix
controls.soWhat: Generic IR plans assume human attackers — agent incidents need agent-shaped containment steps, decided before 2am.

controls.name: Model registry with rollback and deprecation
controls.description: Every production model/prompt configuration is versioned in a registry with one-step rollback and a managed deprecation path for retiring systems.
controls.implementation: Register models, prompts, and configs with approvals; keep last-known-good deployable; define deprecation criteria and user-migration duties.
controls.testingMethod: Execute a rollback drill from a bad deploy; verify a retired system is actually unreachable.
controls.evidenceRequired: Registry records, rollback drill logs, deprecation checklist
controls.soWhat: Fast rollback is the cheapest mitigation for almost every model failure — and deprecation is where zombie systems breed incidents.

controls.name: Business continuity for AI-dependent processes
controls.description: Business processes that depend on AI have continuity plans for model outage, provider suspension, or forced rollback — including human-process fallbacks.
controls.implementation: Identify AI-critical processes from the inventory; document manual/degraded alternatives; set RTOs; test the human fallback annually.
controls.testingMethod: Run a process with the AI disabled for a working session and measure throughput and error.
controls.evidenceRequired: BC plans, fallback test reports, RTO evidence
relatedStandards: CSA AICM: Business Continuity & Operational Resilience
controls.soWhat: When AI becomes the process, its outage is a business outage — CPS 230 already expects this for regulated entities.

controls.name: Vulnerable-user and child-safety safeguards
controls.risk: Safety and trust risk to vulnerable users and children
controls.description: Consumer-facing AI implements age assurance, harm-pattern detection, crisis escalation, abuse reporting, and moderation tuned for vulnerable users and minors.
controls.implementation: Apply eSafety codes: age assurance where required, self-harm and grooming pattern detection with human escalation, visible reporting, and periodic safety audits of conversations.
controls.testingMethod: Safety-test with scripted vulnerable-user scenarios; verify escalation and reporting paths fire.
controls.evidenceRequired: Safety test results, escalation logs, audit reports
controls.soWhat: Companion-bot harms are this year's enforcement theme in Australia — these safeguards are moving from ethics to obligation.

controls.name: Environmental footprint measurement and workforce transition
controls.risk: Societal impact risk: environment and workforce
controls.description: The organisation measures AI compute/energy footprint and manages workforce impacts of AI-driven change with reskilling and consultation.
controls.implementation: Track energy/emissions for material AI workloads and include in sustainability reporting; pair AI-driven role changes with consultation and reskilling budgets.
controls.testingMethod: Reconcile reported AI footprint against cloud billing; audit reskilling uptake for affected roles.
controls.evidenceRequired: Footprint methodology, sustainability report extract, reskilling records
controls.soWhat: Societal impact is the emptiest cell in most control libraries — measurable footprint and workforce-transition evidence fills it credibly.

vendors.name: Palo Alto Networks Prisma AIRS (Protect AI)
vendors.targetCustomer: Enterprise security teams protecting AI applications
vendors.useCase: AI application security posture management, AI-SPM, LLM security assessment, model scanning, runtime protection
vendors.adoptionEvidence: Listed in Modulos 22-vendor buyer's guide 2026. Palo Alto Networks acquired Protect AI and integrated into Prisma Cloud platform.
vendors.strengths: Leverages Palo Alto's enterprise security distribution and existing customer relationships. Integrated with broader cloud security platform. Strong brand trust in enterprise security.
vendors.weaknesses: Integration depth with Prisma Cloud may limit standalone AI security use. Pricing transparency limited. Newer category — reference customers less known.
vendors.differentiators: Existing enterprise security relationship via Palo Alto Networks — easier procurement for existing Prisma Cloud customers. End-to-end AI security across build, deploy, run lifecycle.
vendors.risks: Vendor lock-in for Prisma Cloud customers. Rapidly evolving AI security threat landscape may outpace roadmap.
vendors.maturity: Growth stage — product integrated into established platform
vendors.auRelevance: Medium — available globally, AU enterprise relevance through Palo Alto's AU presence
vendors.enterpriseRelevance: High — targets existing Palo Alto enterprise customers

vendors.name: Credo AI
vendors.targetCustomer: Compliance and GRC teams in regulated industries
vendors.useCase: Regulatory compliance, AI risk management, agent governance
keyFeatures: Centralized AI inventory with shadow AI discovery
Pre-built policy packs (EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001, SOC 2, HITRUST)
Agent governance (pre-deployment testing through runtime action enforcement)
vendors.adoptionEvidence: Listed in IAPP 2026 AI Governance Vendor Report and Modulos 2026 buyer's guide. Named in Gartner AI governance market guides.
vendors.strengths: Forrester Wave Leader; World Economic Forum Technology Pioneer; comprehensive regulatory library
vendors.weaknesses: No inference-pipeline runtime enforcement; steep learning curve; enterprise pricing
vendors.differentiators: Early mover in AI governance with deep regulatory compliance focus. Strong NIST AI RMF alignment.
vendors.risks: Competition from larger platforms (OneTrust, ServiceNow, IBM) entering space. Market consolidation risk.

vendors.name: Bifrost
vendors.useCase: Runtime AI governance at infrastructure layer — policy enforcement in the inference pipeline
keyFeatures: Virtual keys with budget/rate limit/routing per consumer
Enterprise guardrails (AWS Bedrock, Azure Content Safety, GraySwan Cygnal, Patronus AI)
RBAC via OpenID Connect (Okta, Microsoft Entra)
Immutable audit logs (SOC 2, GDPR, HIPAA, ISO 27001)
MCP tool filtering with strict allow-lists
vendors.strengths: 11 microsecond overhead at 5,000 req/s; pipeline-level enforcement (not dashboard-only); open-source

vendors.name: Microsoft Semantic Kernel
vendors.targetCustomer: Developers and enterprises building AI agents and model-connected workflows
vendors.useCase: Connect LLMs to plugins, workflows, vector stores and code/tool execution
vendors.adoptionEvidence: Microsoft’s own security research describes Semantic Kernel as an open-source AI agent framework used to connect AI models to tools, plugins and workflows.
vendors.effectivenessEvidence: Vendor/framework capability evidence is strong, but security effectiveness requires patched versions and secure configuration; Microsoft disclosed and patched CVE-2026-26030 and CVE-2026-25592.
vendors.strengths: Deep Microsoft ecosystem integration; active security research and patching; flexible agent/tool architecture.
vendors.weaknesses: Tool-connected architecture can amplify prompt injection into system actions if developers expose unsafe tools or trust model-controlled parameters.
vendors.differentiators: Microsoft-backed open-source agent framework with enterprise ecosystem alignment.
vendors.risks: Unpatched versions or unsafe plugin design can create host-level RCE or arbitrary file write paths.
vendors.pricing: Open-source SDK; commercial costs depend on Azure/model services and enterprise architecture.

vendors.name: Anthropic
vendors.category: Frontier model provider / safety-focused AI vendor
vendors.targetCustomer: Enterprises, developers, cyber defenders, government users
vendors.useCase: Long-horizon agents, coding, cybersecurity support, and research workflows
vendors.adoptionEvidence: Anthropic now points to public-sector operational use as well as enterprise integration: the Government of Alberta reports heavy use of Claude Code for code review and vulnerability remediation, and Reuters reported a US cyber agency using Anthropic’s Mythos to audit government code.
vendors.effectivenessEvidence: Anthropic’s Alberta case study gives a concrete work-scale signal (466 million lines scanned in 20 hours), but buyers should still validate false positives, containment, and human-review workflows independently.
weaknesses: Temporary access pauses show operational fragility
High-sensitivity models require careful policy design
Retention and access restrictions may complicate deployments
vendors.pricing: Fable 5 listed at $10 input / $50 output per million tokens; other models vary.

vendors.name: OWASP Dependency-Track
vendors.targetCustomer: Security engineering, platform teams, and open-source users
vendors.useCase: SBOM ingestion, dependency inventory, and software supply-chain risk monitoring
vendors.adoptionEvidence: OWASP describes the platform as widely used for software supply-chain risk reduction.
vendors.effectivenessEvidence: The v5 release claims fault tolerance, scalability, and integrity verification, but buyers still need to validate fit and coverage in their own pipeline.
strengths: Better fit for large or resilient deployments after v5 redesign
weaknesses: Only as good as the quality of the supplied SBOMs
differentiators: Redesigned for scale and recovery in v5
risks: False confidence if dependencies are incomplete
vendors.pricing: Open source / no licensing cost for the core project

vendors.name: OpenAI
vendors.useCase: Agentic coding, general-purpose automation, and safety-evaluated model deployment
keyFeatures: Public deployment safety hub and system cards
vendors.adoptionEvidence: Public news and research pages show active productisation of agentic tools and deployment-safety materials; exact enterprise adoption varies by product.
vendors.effectivenessEvidence: OpenAI says deployment simulation improved estimates of undesired behavior and surfaced new misalignment before release; independent validation is still limited.
weaknesses: Safety claims still need independent verification
Model and policy changes can be abrupt
risks: Tool-use abuse if controls are weak
vendors.pricing: Usage-based; exact pricing depends on model and product tier.

vendors.name: OpenAI GPT-5.6 Sol
vendors.targetCustomer: Developers, enterprise buyers, and security teams
vendors.useCase: Coding, science, cybersecurity, and general-purpose assistant workflows
vendors.adoptionEvidence: Vendor preview only; no independent adoption evidence in this cycle.
vendors.effectivenessEvidence: Vendor says performance and safety improved; independent validation pending.
differentiators: Capability and safety are packaged together

vendors.name: Anthropic Claude Tag
vendors.useCase: Slack-native delegation, shared team work, and asynchronous task execution
vendors.adoptionEvidence: Anthropic says 65% of its product team’s code is created by an internal version of Claude Tag.
vendors.effectivenessEvidence: Vendor-reported internal use suggests practical value, but no independent benchmark in this source.
differentiators: Team-shared agent in a collaboration channel
vendors.pricing: Beta; enterprise/team pricing not stated in the source

vendors.name: OpenAI Daybreak
vendors.targetCustomer: Security teams, open-source maintainers, and enterprise defenders
vendors.useCase: Vulnerability discovery, validation, and patch support
vendors.adoptionEvidence: Announcement and product messaging only in this cycle; no independent adoption evidence yet.
vendors.effectivenessEvidence: Vendor says the package helps teams find, validate, and patch vulnerabilities at scale.
differentiators: Security workflow bundled with frontier-model capability

vendors.name: DXC Technology
vendors.targetCustomer: Large enterprises, regulated industries, and government customers
vendors.useCase: Integrating Claude into regulated operational systems and managed services
vendors.adoptionEvidence: Anthropic says DXC already uses Claude across its own operations and OASIS platform.
vendors.effectivenessEvidence: Anthropic reports 95%+ of OASIS code was generated by Claude and reviewed by engineers; self-reported vendor evidence only.

vendors.name: OWASP GenAI Security Project
vendors.targetCustomer: Security teams, builders, red-teamers, governance groups
vendors.useCase: Agentic AI risk frameworks, secure deployment guidance, and security baselines
keyFeatures: State of Agentic AI Security and Governance
AI security resources and cheat sheets
vendors.adoptionEvidence: The framework is publicly peer-reviewed and backed by a broad contributor base; it is increasingly cited in security commentary and product guidance.
vendors.effectivenessEvidence: The project provides concrete, operational risk categories for agentic systems, but it is a framework rather than a product and does not itself enforce controls.
strengths: Well aligned to current agentic risks
weaknesses: Requires translation into internal policy and tooling
risks: Can be treated as guidance without enforcement
Framework drift as threat patterns change

vendors.name: OpenAI Frontier
vendors.targetCustomer: Enterprises building and governing AI agents
vendors.useCase: Deploying AI coworkers with shared context, permissions, onboarding, and workflow controls
vendors.adoptionEvidence: OpenAI says Frontier has first customers across manufacturing, finance, energy, and technology, and that more than 1 million businesses have been using AI products over the past few years.
vendors.effectivenessEvidence: Vendor-provided evidence only; the product narrative is strong on enterprise workflow control but should be validated through customer references and independent testing.
vendors.strengths: Clear enterprise packaging for agent workflows, governance, and permissions.
vendors.weaknesses: Vendor marketing evidence is limited and production effectiveness depends on implementation discipline.
vendors.differentiators: Positions governance and permissions as first-class features rather than add-ons.
vendors.risks: Concentration risk and policy dependence on a single frontier vendor.

vendors.name: Claude Fable 5
vendors.targetCustomer: Enterprises and regulated industries using Claude
vendors.useCase: General-purpose and regulated-industry AI with safety gating and access controls
keyFeatures: Claude Platform / Claude.ai / Claude Code / Claude Cowork support
vendors.adoptionEvidence: Anthropic says access was restored globally on 1 July 2026 and that Fable 5 is available across multiple Claude surfaces.
vendors.effectivenessEvidence: Anthropic says it updated safeguards after a reported cybersecurity bypass and is working on a shared jailbreak-severity framework with major cloud partners.
vendors.strengths: Strong security framing and explicit safeguard narrative.
vendors.weaknesses: The product is still subject to policy-driven access changes.
vendors.differentiators: Access gating and safeguard updates are front and centre.
vendors.risks: Export-control and access-policy changes can affect availability.
vendors.pricing: Subscription and usage-credit model; verify directly with vendor.
vendors.auRelevance: High for regulated industries and security teams.

vendors.name: Anthropic Claude Code Security
vendors.useCase: AI-assisted vulnerability discovery and patch suggestions for codebases
vendors.adoptionEvidence: Anthropic’s Alberta case study shows the tool family being used for large-scale government code review rather than only internal experimentation.
vendors.effectivenessEvidence: The public claim is specific and operational: 466 million lines of code were scanned in 20 hours, and security gaps were remediated across a large government environment.
vendors.strengths: Strong fit for defender workflows and high-volume code review.
vendors.weaknesses: Still a preview product; value depends on triage discipline and secure tooling boundaries.
vendors.differentiators: Reasoning-first scanning and human-review workflow rather than pattern-only scanning.
vendors.risks: Could create overreliance or noise if deployed without clear review and escalation rules.

vendors.name: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026
vendors.useCase: Agentic AI risk taxonomy, control planning, and red-team prioritisation
keyFeatures: Crosswalks to related AI security guidance
integrations: Can be mapped into SDLC and GRC workflows
vendors.adoptionEvidence: The project describes the framework as globally peer-reviewed and explicitly aligned to operational AI-agent security needs.
vendors.effectivenessEvidence: High-value as a control baseline; effectiveness depends on local implementation and testing.
vendors.strengths: Clear, operationally useful taxonomy for agentic systems.
vendors.weaknesses: Not a product and not a compliance standard by itself.
vendors.differentiators: Focused specifically on agentic applications rather than general LLM safety.
vendors.risks: Teams may treat the checklist as complete when it is only a starting point.

vendors.name: LatticeFlow AI
vendors.targetCustomer: Enterprises and regulated organisations deploying GenAI apps and agents
vendors.useCase: AI governance, technical evaluation, agent risk reporting, adversarial testing, and compliance evidence
vendors.adoptionEvidence: Homepage cites customer stories for Axpo, PastaHR, and banking/critical infrastructure use cases, and positions the company as a governance-platform vendor for agentic AI.
vendors.effectivenessEvidence: The company says its platform turns AI governance into executable controls with live visibility and technical risk reports; independent benchmark evidence was not verified in this run.
vendors.strengths: Technical-eval focus, explicit agentic AI positioning, and evidence-oriented messaging rather than checklist-only governance.
vendors.weaknesses: Claims are largely vendor-provided; independent customer outcome data and pricing are not publicly verified here.
vendors.differentiators: Combines live visibility, scalable evaluations, adversarial testing, and risk evidence in one platform.
vendors.risks: Vendor marketing may outpace independently verified effectiveness; integration and adoption effort may be material for enterprise buyers.
vendors.auRelevance: Medium — useful to Australian enterprises and regulators looking for executable AI assurance tooling.

regulations.title: EU Artificial Intelligence Act (Regulation 2024/1689)
regulations.summary: The AI Act now has a fresh transparency-guidelines consultation layered on top of the existing 2 August 2026 timeline; the Digital Omnibus relief story remains conditional on formal adoption.
regulations.affected: Providers, deployers, importers and distributors of AI systems in or affecting the EU, including GPAI providers and users of high-risk AI systems.
regulations.obligations: Risk classification, prohibited practice compliance, high-risk risk management and documentation, transparency obligations and GPAI-related transparency/copyright/safety requirements as applicable.
regulations.timeline: Prohibited-practice rules effective February 2025; further obligations phase in through 2026 and beyond.
regulations.enforcementRisk: High for EU-facing providers/deployers as obligations mature.
regulations.australiaRelevance: Medium-High for Australian exporters, multinational compliance teams and benchmark regulatory design.

regulations.title: Executive Order 14365 — Unified National AI Policy
regulations.summary: Directs federal agencies to develop unified national AI policy approach, evaluate state AI laws for conflicts with federal policy, condition federal funding on state compliance, and challenge certain state AI laws through legal action. Exempts state laws on child safety, data center infrastructure, and state government procurement.
affected: AI companies operating in the US
obligations: Federal agencies: develop unified AI policy
regulations.timeline: Signed December 2025. Implementation ongoing.
regulations.enforcementRisk: Medium — depends on legal challenges to pre-emption approach
regulations.australiaRelevance: Medium — influences global AI regulatory direction

regulations.title: TAKE IT DOWN Act (Targeting AI-Generated Exploitation)
regulations.summary: Federal legislation targeting AI-generated deepfakes, particularly non-consensual intimate imagery. Establishes criminal penalties for creation and distribution of AI-generated exploitative content.
obligations: Criminal liability for AI-generated exploitative content
regulations.timeline: Passed 2025
regulations.enforcementRisk: Medium
regulations.australiaRelevance: Medium — mirrors similar Australian deepfake/online safety concerns

regulations.title: Proposed Mandatory AI Guardrails for High-Risk AI
regulations.summary: Proposals Paper published September 2024 outlining potential mandatory guardrails for high-risk AI: model testing, transparency, human oversight, record-keeping. Building on the Voluntary AI Safety Standard (August 2024). No legislation enacted yet. Existing statutes (Privacy Act 1988, Corporations Act 2001, Online Safety Act 2021) apply to AI use.
affected: AI service providers to Australian entities
regulations.timeline: Consultation ongoing. Legislation anticipated but no timeline confirmed.
regulations.enforcementRisk: Medium-High — depends on legislative progress
regulations.australiaRelevance: Critical — directly governs Australian AI deployment

regulations.title: California Senate Bill 243 — AI Companion Chatbot Regulation
regulations.summary: First US state law regulating AI companion chatbots. Requires mandatory AI disclosure, protocols to prevent harmful content (suicide, self-harm, sexually explicit material for minors), annual reporting to state authorities. Creates a private right of action allowing injured individuals to sue for damages. Enacted October 2025, effective January 1, 2026.
affected: Social media platforms with AI chat features
Character.AI, Replika, OpenAI, and similar providers
obligations: Mandatory AI disclosure — users must be informed they are interacting with AI, not human
Protocols to prevent harmful content for minors (suicide, self-harm, sexually explicit)
Annual reporting to state authorities on safety measures
Private right of action for injured individuals
regulations.timeline: Enacted October 2025. Effective January 1, 2026.
regulations.enforcementRisk: High — private right of action enables litigation regardless of state enforcement
regulations.australiaRelevance: High — Australian regulators (eSafety, OAIC) are monitoring AI companion chatbot safety. Similar regulation plausible.

regulations.title: Policy for the responsible use of AI in government (DTA)
regulations.summary: Australia's AI governance baseline remains the DTA policy/register architecture, while Reuters reported that government is preparing an AI office to coordinate regulation.
regulations.affected: All non-corporate Commonwealth entities (mandatory); corporate Commonwealth entities (encouraged); excludes defence portfolio and national intelligence community
obligations: Foundational AI training for all APS staff
Accountable owner for each AI use case

Reporting pathways for staff and public
regulations.timeline: First requirements effective June 15, 2026; all remaining by December 2026
regulations.enforcementRisk: Low-Medium — government internal policy, not legislation
regulations.australiaRelevance: High — directly applicable to all Australian Government agencies

regulations.title: OAIC guidance on privacy and commercially available AI products
regulations.summary: OAIC guidance clarifies that Privacy Act and APP obligations apply to deploying AI systems involving personal information, including internal and customer-facing uses of generative and general-purpose AI products.
regulations.affected: APP entities and government agencies deploying AI products involving personal information
regulations.obligations: Assess necessity; practise privacy by design; manage transparency, accuracy and explainability; avoid entering personal or sensitive information into public AI chatbots as a matter of best practice; review vendor data handling and retention.

regulations.enforcementRisk: Medium-High where AI deployment involves personal information or adverse impacts.

regulations.title: EU AI Act General-Purpose AI Code of Practice
regulations.summary: The GPAI Code of Practice is a voluntary tool to demonstrate compliance with AI Act Articles 53 and 55 until harmonised standards are developed. It covers transparency, copyright, and safety/security for systemic-risk GPAI models.
regulations.affected: Providers of general-purpose AI models and downstream providers integrating GPAI models into systems
regulations.obligations: Providers using the Code can demonstrate compliance with documentation, downstream information, training-data summary, copyright policy, and systemic-risk evaluation/mitigation obligations; providers not using it must prove compliance by alternative means.
regulations.timeline: GPAI provisions applied from 2 August 2025; Commission enforcement begins 2 August 2026; pre-August-2025 models have until 2 August 2027.
regulations.enforcementRisk: High for GPAI providers in EU market as enforcement starts.
regulations.australiaRelevance: Medium-High for Australian model providers or deployers entering EU markets.

deadlines.label: Commission enforcement of GPAI obligations begins

regulations.soWhat: GPAI providers should map Code-of-Practice commitments to evidence they can produce before enforcement starts in August 2026.

regulations.title: APP 1 automated decision-making transparency obligation
regulations.summary: OAIC guidance and ACAPS 2026 reinforce that the APP 1 ADM transparency obligation is now a concrete compliance project: privacy policies must describe the kinds of personal information used and the kinds of decisions made, and Australians are increasingly wary of training AI systems on their personal information.
regulations.affected: APP entities using computer programs, including AI or ADM systems, in decisions that significantly affect individuals.
regulations.obligations: Where criteria are met, disclose kinds of personal information used, kinds of decisions made solely by computer programs, and kinds of decisions where a computer program does something substantially and directly related to making the decision.
regulations.timeline: Consultation closes 15 June 2026; OAIC guidance expected September 2026; obligation commences 10 December 2026.
regulations.enforcementRisk: Medium-High from commencement, especially for high-impact consumer, employment, credit, insurance and government services.
regulations.australiaRelevance: High

regulations.title: EU Digital Omnibus on AI (AI Act simplification)
regulations.summary: First set of amendments to the EU AI Act since adoption in June 2024. Postpones high-risk AI system obligations (Annex III from 2 August 2026 to 2 December 2027; Annex I from 2 August 2027 to 2 August 2028). Postpones Article 50(2) synthetic-content transparency obligations from 2 August 2026 to 2 December 2026. Postpones national regulatory sandbox obligations from 2 August 2026 to 2 August 2027. Realigns AI Act with sectoral product safety rules; moves Machinery Regulation from Annex I Section A to Section B, shifting from dual-compliance to sectoral primacy. Adds new prohibited practices. Part of broader 'Omnibus VII' simplification package.
affected: Product-safety regulators (machinery, radio equipment, lifts, medical devices)
obligations: Continue compliance with in-force AI Act obligations (prohibited practices, GPAI rules, transparency for in-scope systems)
Prepare for high-risk obligations under new dates (Annex III 2 Dec 2027, Annex I 2 Aug 2028)
Comply with Article 50(2) synthetic-content transparency from 2 December 2026
Track formal adoption of Digital Omnibus before 2 August 2026
regulations.timeline: Provisional deal 7 May 2026; formal adoption targeted before 2 August 2026; new dates take effect on adoption
regulations.enforcementRisk: High — provisional status creates uncertainty; some obligations remain in force regardless
regulations.australiaRelevance: High — Australian organisations with EU operations or customers are directly affected. Multinationals must continue AI Act readiness while benefiting from deferral.

events.what: Digital Omnibus on AI / AI Act simplification measures

regulations.title: Executive Order on Promoting Advanced Artificial Intelligence Innovation and Security (2 June 2026)
regulations.summary: Establishes a federal AI cybersecurity clearinghouse (NSA, CISA, DoD, National Cyber Director), directs federal cyber defence uplift within 30 days, and requires a 60-day benchmarking regime to designate 'covered frontier models' and design a voluntary review framework for AI developers. Section 4 covers export control, IP protection and innovation. Implementation details depend on forthcoming CISA and OMB directives.
affected: All US-based AI vendors serving federal procurement
obligations: Engage with voluntary federal pre-release frontier model review (Section 3)
Comply with binding operational directives from CISA on AI-enabled cyber defence (Section 2)
Track covered frontier model thresholds (60 days from 2 June)
Engage with AI cybersecurity clearinghouse vulnerability disclosure (Section 2)
regulations.timeline: Effective 2 June 2026; 30-day cyber defence directives; 60-day benchmarking and covered-frontier-model threshold
regulations.enforcementRisk: Medium — voluntary framework, but procurement access and federal contractor status depend on engagement; subsequent binding operational directives may impose hard obligations on critical infrastructure
regulations.australiaRelevance: Medium — Australian AI suppliers selling into US federal procurement may need to engage with the voluntary review; DTA may examine the order when updating the AU Government AI policy

regulations.title: CMA Conduct Requirements on Google Search — AI publisher opt-out (3 June 2026)
regulations.summary: UK Competition and Markets Authority imposed new conduct requirements on Google Search under the UK's digital markets regime. Google must give UK publishers the ability to opt out of content being used in AI Overviews, AI Mode, AI model training, fine-tuning, and AI grounding without losing conventional search ranking. The CMA called this a 'world-first' requirement; Google began testing the controls the same day and said it plans to roll them out globally.
obligations: Provide publisher opt-out for AI Overviews, AI Mode, grounding, training and fine-tuning
Cite publisher content in AI-generated search with clear links
Apply controls to all changes to AI search features
regulations.timeline: Issued 3 June 2026; nine-month compliance window per the CMA; Google began testing the same day
regulations.enforcementRisk: High for non-compliance — binding conduct requirement under UK digital markets regime; enforcement can include fines and functional separation
regulations.australiaRelevance: High — Australian publishers benefit; the ACCC's digital platforms regime and News Media Bargaining Code are the Australian analogues; expected to inform ACCC and Treasury policy on AI training data and content licensing

regulations.title: APRA supervisory expectations on AI risk management and governance

regulations.timeline: The regulatory signal is already active; APRA’s MFA clarification is a current implementation prompt for regulated entities.
regulations.enforcementRisk: High
regulations.australiaRelevance: Very high

standards.name: NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)
standards.issuer: National Institute of Standards and Technology (US)
standards.description: Voluntary framework providing guidance for AI risk management across four functions: Govern, Map, Measure, Manage.

standards.name: Voluntary AI Safety Standard (Australia)
standards.description: Released August 2024. Voluntary standard with guardrails for high-risk AI including model testing, transparency, human oversight, and record-keeping. Precursor to proposed mandatory guardrails.

standards.name: OECD AI Principles
standards.description: A cross-border governance baseline covering transparency, robustness, accountability, and human-centred values.

standards.name: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications
standards.description: Industry standard for LLM application security risks including prompt injection, data leakage, supply chain vulnerabilities, and insecure output handling.

standards.name: California SB 243 AI Companion Chatbot Safety Requirements
standards.description: Regulatory framework establishing mandatory safety requirements for AI companion chatbots including disclosure, harmful content prevention for minors, annual reporting, and private right of action.

standards.name: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2026)
standards.description: Updated list of 10 critical LLM application security vulnerabilities. Prompt injection remains #1. Added new categories for emerging attack patterns including MCP server attacks, tool-use vulnerabilities, and agentic AI risks. 73% of production deployments found vulnerable to prompt injection.

standards.name: Gartner Proportional AI Agent Governance Framework
standards.description: Framework classifying AI agents by autonomy level: observe (read-only), assist (suggest), act (execute within scope), autonomous (independent operation). Each level has distinct trust boundaries and governance requirements. Warns against binary governance approaches.

standards.name: NIST Privacy Framework 1.1
standards.description: Updated Privacy Framework (April 2025) specifically addressing AI privacy risk management. Designed to work alongside NIST AI RMF and CSF 2.0. Provides structured approach to managing AI privacy risks.

standards.name: NIST AI Risk Management Framework
standards.description: A voluntary framework for governing and managing AI risk across mapping, measuring, managing, and governing functions.

standards.name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile
standards.description: Profile extending the NIST AI RMF for generative AI risks, including controls such as impact assessments, incident response, monitoring, data provenance, secure software development and synthetic content labelling.

standards.name: Frontier AI Safety Framework Components
standards.description: Issue brief identifying five core components for frontier AI safety frameworks: risk identification, capability/risk thresholds, assessments, mitigation and governance.

standards.name: DTA Agentic AI addendum to the AI technical standard
standards.description: Addendum setting expectations for Australian Government agencies designing, deploying, managing and operating agentic AI systems with increased autonomy while maintaining human oversight, accountability and control.

standards.name: NIST AI 800-2 Initial Public Draft — Practices for Automated Benchmark Evaluations of Language Models
standards.issuer: NIST / Center for AI Standards and Innovation
standards.description: Voluntary practices for automated benchmark evaluations of language models and AI agent systems covering measurement targets, implementation, analysis and reporting.

standards.name: National AI Centre Guidance for AI Adoption — Implementation Guidance
standards.description: Six essential practices for complex and higher-risk AI use: accountability, impact planning, risk management, information sharing, test/monitor and human control.

standards.name: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026
standards.description: Peer-reviewed guidance for identifying and mitigating the most important risks in autonomous and agentic AI systems.

standards.name: OWASP Agentic Skills Top 10 (in development)
standards.description: Proposed Top 10 security risks in agentic AI skills across OpenClaw (SKILL.md YAML), Claude Code (skill.json), Cursor/Codex (manifest.json), and VS Code (package.json) ecosystems. v1.0 expected Q3 2026. Layered defense approach: MCP Top 10 (protocol) → Agentic Skills Top 10 (skill content) → LLM/Agentic Top 10 (models).

standards.name: NIST AI 800-2 — Practices for Automated Benchmark Evaluations of Language Models (Initial Public Draft)
standards.issuer: NIST Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI)
standards.description: Initial public draft published 30 January 2026. Documents preliminary best practices for evaluating language models and AI agent systems. Primary audience: technical staff at organisations evaluating AI systems, including AI deployers, developers, and third-party evaluators. Reflects CAISI's experience partnering with leading US AI organisations to evaluate frontier AI models. Public comment closed 31 March 2026.

standards.name: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025
standards.description: A security risk taxonomy for LLM applications covering prompt injection, sensitive data disclosure, supply chain, and other high-frequency failure modes.

standards.name: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications for 2026
standards.description: Peer-reviewed framework highlighting the most critical security risks facing autonomous and agentic AI systems, including tool misuse, identity, and orchestration-layer threats.

standards.name: Agentic AI addendum to the AI technical standard
standards.description: Australian government guidance for designing and operating agentic AI systems with human oversight and control.

standards.name: AI Risk Management Framework
standards.description: A voluntary framework for governing, mapping, measuring, and managing AI risk.

standards.name: MITRE ATT&CK
standards.description: A knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques used to classify and detect malicious activity; Anthropic mapped AI-enabled cyber activity to ATT&CK in its June 2026 analysis.

standards.name: CycloneDX
standards.description: An SBOM standard used to inventory software components and support dependency and integrity verification in tooling such as OWASP Dependency-Track.

standards.name: OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications
standards.description: Community-maintained guidance on the major security risks facing LLM applications.

standards.name: AI Risk Management Framework 1.0
standards.description: A lifecycle framework for governing, mapping, measuring, and managing AI risk.

standards.name: ISO/IEC 42001
standards.description: An AI management system standard for establishing, implementing, maintaining and continually improving AI governance controls.

standards.description: NIST initiative aimed at interoperable and secure AI agents.
